text: So this is the versater morcellator which I have a personal preference for because it is a very surgeon friendly morcellator lightweight. This is something similar to the gynecare that I was using for a very long time. The assembly just involves me putting in the beta dip to a fifteen mm port. You remember infertility patient she had a very strong rectus sheath. So we stretch, change signal, we stretch with the ten mm port first. having a lot of force required. So I am going to stretch the rector sheath with a scissor. Open the scissor. This is the easiest way to stretch without causing too much of bleeding. And then we place the blunt trocar of the morcellator port.

The surgeon adjusts the morcellator from safe mode to cut mode using a foot switch, then switches from a grasper to a tenaculum for better grip on a fibroid. The tenaculum provides a secure hold without hitting the blade, allowing effective cutting of the fibroid tissue.

The surgeon uses a new morselator blade to effectively cut the fibroid. Alternative removal methods include bisecting the fibroid with two tooth needle holders and extracting it through a ten mm trocar or performing a colpotomy from the posterior pouch for removal.
